interactions.prompt: I need to design a failure mode for AdaGrad. Since the algorithm accumulates squared gradients forever in the denominator, if I inject a single massive outlier into the dataset during training, will it permanently kill the learning rate? How does this connect to the assumptions I wrote in Task 1.2?
interactions.response_summary: The LLM confirmed that a massive outlier will cause the gradient to explode for one step, permanently inflating the denominator and shrinking the learning rate to zero. It helped connect this to Assumption 3.

interactions.prompt: Can you help me write the code to artificially inject a massive outlier at epoch 20 to demonstrate this failure mode? I want to plot the loss curve flatlining to prove the model goes blind.
interactions.response_summary: The LLM provided a modified training loop that multiplies a batch of gradients by a massive scalar at a specific epoch, demonstrating the permanent flatline effect on the loss curve.
interactions.student_modification: I adjusted the size of the outlier multiplier and the epoch at which it is introduced to make the flatline look more dramatic on my specific matplotlib graph.

declaration: I declare that the above prompts were used to hypothesize a structural vulnerability in the algorithm and to figure out how to programmatically simulate that failure in a controlled experiment.
